Atelectasis.explanation: Pleural fluid can promote compressive atelectatic change.

Cardiomegaly.explanation: Cardiac enlargement with vascular congestion can support cardiogenic edema.

Effusion.explanation: Parapneumonic pleural effusions can accompany pulmonary infection.

Infiltration.explanation: Both findings can represent increased lung opacity from inflammatory causes.

Mass.explanation: Masses and nodules share differential pathways and follow-up imaging strategies.

Nodule.explanation: Enlarging pulmonary nodules may evolve into larger mass lesions over time.

Pneumonia.explanation: Airspace consolidation is a common radiographic expression of pneumonia.
Infectious pleural inflammation can produce a parapneumonic effusion.

Pneumothorax.explanation: Large or tension pneumothorax can rapidly compromise ventilation and circulation.

Consolidation.explanation: Focal consolidation with compatible symptoms supports infectious etiology.

Edema.explanation: Pulmonary edema and cardiomegaly together favor cardiac decompensation.

Emphysema.explanation: Bullous lung changes in emphysema can predispose to secondary pneumothorax.

Fibrosis.explanation: Chronic pleuroparenchymal processes can produce both fibrosis and pleural thickening.

Pleural_thickening.explanation: Long-standing pleural disease may coexist with adjacent fibrotic change.

Hernia.explanation: A hiatal hernia can present as a retrocardiac opacity or air-fluid level.

No finding.explanation: No dominant acute abnormal pattern was selected by the model.
